goal: Suggest preprocessing and feature-engineering actions for predicting card-level loyalty score from Elo card metadata plus historical and new-merchant transaction logs under the competition RMSE metric. This benchmark slice scores card-age features, transaction encodings, and card-level aggregations rather than merchant-table enrichment.

task_characteristics.analysis_notes: Large multi-table finance regression task centered on card_id, with a 29,112,361-row historical transaction table plus a smaller but still substantial new-merchant table and an auxiliary merchant lookup table. The broader competition data remains moderately wide at a 34-column case-insensitive union, but the curated benchmark slice intentionally narrows to 14 core preprocessing actions plus 2 low-support model-specific aggregation variants. The scored path focuses on card-age features, transaction encodings, and card-level relational aggregation rather than merchant-table enrichment, and its repeated date-derived features are now scored through self-contained composite units rather than standalone parse steps.

overview.title: Elo Merchant Category Recommendation
overview.summary: Can you identify the most relevant merchant opportunities by predicting each cardholder's future loyalty score?
overview.raw_text: Elo built partnerships with merchants to offer promotions and discounts to cardholders, but irrelevant offers hurt the customer experience. The competition asks participants to predict a continuous loyalty score for each card_id by uncovering signal in anonymized card metadata, historical transactions, new merchant transactions, and broader merchant context. The benchmark slice here narrows to the repeated notebook core: engineer card-age features from first_active_month, encode recurring transaction flags and categories, and aggregate transaction behavior back to card_id before fitting a regressor. Submissions are evaluated by root mean squared error on the target loyalty score.

dataset_description.summary: The downloadable competition bundle consists of a card-level train/test pair keyed by card_id, two large transaction fact tables (historical and new merchant transactions), a merchant lookup table merchants.csv, and sample_submission.csv. Public notebook close reading for this benchmark slice consistently focuses on the card table plus the two transaction logs: train/test contribute first_active_month and three anonymized base card features, while the transaction tables contribute purchase timing, purchase amount, installments, month lag, merchant/location ids, and repeated categorical flags. The benchmark keeps merchants.csv as part of the raw competition package, but the scored action bank intentionally emphasizes card-age features, transaction encodings, and card-level aggregates rather than merchant-attribute joins.
dataset_description.raw_text: The Elo competition data is organized around one row per card in train and test plus two transaction logs and one merchant lookup table. train.csv and test.csv each include card_id, first_active_month, and three anonymized base card features; train also includes the continuous loyalty target. historical_transactions.csv records prior card activity, while new_merchant_transactions.csv records activity at merchants not seen in the historical window. Both transaction tables contain purchase timing, authorization and category flags, purchase amount, installments, month lag, and multiple merchant/location ids keyed by card_id. merchants.csv provides merchant-level categorical attributes and lagged sales and purchase summary statistics keyed by merchant_id, but the curated benchmark slice does not score merchant-table joins. Instead, it centers on date-derived card-age and recency features, repeated Y/N and category encodings, and aggregating each transaction table back to card_id before fitting a card-level regressor.
